MATH 464: LECTURE 1
RICE UNIVERSITY, SPRING 2020

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
label: Categories
body:
In this course we’ll adopt a utilitarian point of view when it comes to category theory: while many
people study it for its own sake, we’ll think of category theory as a clean, organizing framework for
a lot of modern mathematics, that allows us to relate seemingly di↵erent parts of the subject.

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
label: Initial and terminal objects
body:
Some categories are endowed with particular objects that either admit a map to or from ev-
ery other object in the category. I want to use these objects to introduce an important notion:
uniqueness up to unique isomorphism.

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
body:
An initial object in a category is an object P Ob( ) such that for every other
C 2 C
object A Ob( ) there exists a unique morphism P A (i.e., Mor(P, A) is a singleton set for
2 C !
every A). A terminal object is an object P Ob( ) such that for every other object A Ob( )
2 C 2 C
there exists a unique morphism A P (i.e., Mor(A, P ) is a singleton set for every A).
!

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
body:
The category Sets has an initial object: the empty set. It also has terminal objects:
any singleton set is a terminal object. So these objects need not be unique. However, it’s clear that
any two singleton sets are in bijection by a unique map.

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
body:
In the category Groups, any group with exactly one element is both an initial and
a terminal object!

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
body:
In the category Rings, the integers Z give an initial object. If A is any ring, then
in order for a map � : Z A to be a ring homomorphism, we must map 1 Z to 1 A. The property
! 2
that �(a + b) = �(a) + �(b) then forces your hand for every other integer. Thus � is unique! The
A
zero ring is a terminal object.

title: Lecture 1: 1. Categories
body:
Initial and/or terminal objects need not exist. For example, in the category of all
fields, there are neither initial objects nor terminal objects.
2

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
MATH 464: LECTURE 2
RICE UNIVERSITY, SPRING 2020

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
We alluded in class to a uniqueness property enjoyed by initial and terminal objects, whenever
they exist. Here it is. The proof of the following lemma is very simple, but it comes up over and
over again in all branches of Mathematics, so make sure you have really internalized it.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
Initial and terminal objects, whenever they exist, are unique up to unique isomor-
phism, i.e., any two objects that are initial (or terminal) are isomorphic to each other, and this
isomorphism itself is unique.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
We’ll show the claim for initial objects. Suppose that P and Q are two initial objects in a
category C. The idea of the proof is to “play the objects against each other”.
Since P is initial, we know there is a unique morphism φ : P → Q. On the other hand, since Q
is initial we know there is a unique morphism ψ : Q → P . Consider the map φ ◦ ψ : Q → Q. Since
Q is initial we know there is only one map Q → Q, which must be φ ◦ ψ. On the other hand, we
know by the definition of a category that there is a morphism id : Q → Q. Thus, it must be the
Q
case that φ ◦ ψ = id . Similarly, we can show that ψ ◦ φ = id . Thus φ and ψ are isomorphisms,
Q P
and they are unique. (cid:3)

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Maps between categories: Functors
body:
It is conceivable that a category is itself an object of a larger category. If that’s the case, then
there would have to be a notion of “morphisms between categories.” Such a notion exists and is
called a functor. You have seen many functors already in your mathematical life: you just didn’t
know you were staring at them!

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
Let C and D be two categories. A (covariant) functor F : C → D is a rule that
assigns
• to an object A ∈ Ob(C), an object F (A) ∈ Ob(D), and
• to a morphism f : A → B of C, a morphism F (f ) : F (A) → F (B) in D.
[the functor is contravariant if it reverses the direction of a morphism instead of preserving it: to
a morphism f : A → B in C, it assigns a morphism F (f ) : F (B) → F (A).] This assignment must
satisfy the following properties:
(1) F (id ) = id , and
A F (A)
(2) F (g ◦ f ) = F (g) ◦ F (f ) if F is covariant, or F (g ◦ f ) = F (f ) ◦ F (g) if F is contravariant.
Date: January 15th, 2020.
1

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
The forgetful functor U : Groups → Sets. Here U (G) = G, the latter being
considered as a set, i.e., we forget the group structure of G. We also have U (f : G → H) = f : G →
H, the latter being thought of as a map of sets instead of as a group homomorphism.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
The free functor F : Sets → Groups. Here F (S) is the free group generated by
the elements of S. A map F (S) → G is determined by where it sends the generators of F (S), i.e.,
the elements of S. Thus, a map S → T of sets gives rise to a map F (S) → F (T ) between free
groups.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: For those students who have taken Math 444
body:
Consider the category pt-Top
of pointed topological spaces. Here an object is a topological space X together with a marked
point x ∈ X. A map f : (X, x ) → (Y, y ) is a continuous map of topological spaces sending x
0 0 0 0
to y . The fundamental group is a functor π : pt-Top → Groups assigning π (X, x ) to the
0 1 1 0
pointed topological space (X, x ). A map f : (X, x ) → (Y, y ) induces a homomorphism of groups
0 0 0
π (X, x ) → π (Y, y ), so π is a covariant functor.
1 0 1 0 1

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
The functor F : k-Vect → k-Vect where F sends a vector space to its dual space,
that is, F (V ) = Hom(V, k) = V ∗. This is a contravariant functor: you can check that a linear
transformation f : V → W gives rise to F (f ) : W ∗ → V ∗.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Ideal Theory beyond Math 463/563
body:
We’re going to leave category theory aside for a bit. Please spend some time internalizing the
above definitions. Also: think of (and/or look up on the web) examples of initial/terminal objects,
as well as functors.
I’m going to assume you know a little bit about rings, ideals and modules from Math 463/563.
Please read Chapter 1 of Atiyah-Macdonald. I will only go over the material I think many of you
have not been exposed to.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Zorn’s Lemma and maximal ideals. As always, our rings will be commutative, with a
body:
unit. Let A be a ring; recall that a proper ideal p ⊆ A is prime if A/p is an integral domain;
equivalently, if xy ∈ p and x ∈/ p, then y ∈ p. A proper ideal m ⊆ A is maximal if A/m is a field.
Equivalently, an m ideal is maximal if m ⊆ a =⇒ a = A for any ideal a of A, i.e., m is maximal
with respect to inclusion.
Maximal ideals are prime. Here’s a basic question: are we always guaranteed to have maximal
ideals? The answer is yes, but unless we put some restrictions on A, we’ll have to appeal to the
axiom of choice, in the form of Zorn’s lemma.
Recall that a partially ordered set S is a set with a relation ≤ which is reflexive (x ≤ x for all
x ∈ S), transitive (if x ≤ y and y ≤ z then x ≤ z) and with the property that, together, the
statements x ≤ y and y ≤ x imply that x = y. A subset T ⊆ S is a chain if for every pair of
elements x and y in T we have either x ≤ y or y ≤ x. An upper bound on a chain is an element
x ∈ S such that t ≤ x for every t ∈ T . Note that the upper bound itself need not be part of the
chain.
2
Axiom (Zorn’s Lemma). Let S be a non-empty partially ordered set. If every chain of S has an
upper bound, then S has at least one maximal element, i.e., an element s ∈ S such that if for some
x ∈ S we have s ≤ x, then s = x.
This statement is equivalent to the axiom of choice. If you are curious about this, take a look at
Paul Halmos’ book Naive Set Theory.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body: There exist rings with exactly one maximal ideal (e.g., fields, but this is kind of a silly

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
). Such rings (A, m) are called local rings. The field k := A/m is called the residue field of
the local ring.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Nilradical and Jacobson radical. The Nilradical and the Jacobson radical of a ring A are
body: two ideals that come up often in algebra. They make an appearance in Nakayama’s Lemma, a

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
label: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
at the heart of many proofs in Commutative Algebra.
Let N ⊆ A be the set of nilpotent elements of A, i.e,
N := {x ∈ A : xn = 0 for some n > 0}.
This set is in fact an ideal of A because
• if x ∈ N then ax ∈ N for every a ∈ A. This is obvious, but it uses the commutativity of A!
• if x and y are in N then so is x + y: say xn = ym = 0. Then (x + y)n+m−1 = 0.
We call N the nilradical of A. The quotient ring A/N has trivial nilradical.

title: Lecture 2: 1. Uniquness of initial and terminal objects
body:
The ring Z has no nilpotent elements, other than 0, so N = (0). On the other hand,
Z/nZ has nilpotent elements. You can check that in this case N is a principal ideal, generated by
(cid:81)
the class of p, where p runs over the prime divisors of n.
p|n

every prime ideal p, we have f n ∈ p. Since p is prime, this means that f ∈ p.

If instead of intersecting all prime ideals of A, we intersect only those ideals which are maximal,
we obtain a possibly bigger ideal than the nilradical. This is called the Jacobson radical of A, and
is denoted R:

Here’s an interesting example. Suppose that X is a topological space. Let A =
C(X) be the ring of continuous, complex valued functions on X. I claim that R contains only the
zero function. To see this, for every x ∈ X, consider the ideal m := {f ∈ C(X) : f (x) = 0}. I
x
claim this ideal is maximal (prove this!). If f ∈ R, then we must have f ∈ m for every x ∈ X.
x
This means that f (x) = 0 for every x ∈ X. Since N ⊆ R, we conclude that the nilradical in this

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
body:
A priori, the Jacobson radical of a ring could be larger than the nilradical. Here is
an example of where this happens. Let (A, m) be a local ring which is an integral domain (e.g., the
p-adic numbers). Then N = (0) but R = m.
There is a way to characterize the elements of the Jacobson radical, analogous to the character-
ization of elements of the nilradical as nilpotent elements

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
body: We have x ∈ R if and only if 1 − xy is a unit for every y ∈ A.

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
label: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
body:
Suppose that 1 − xy is not a unit for some y ∈ A. Then 1 − xy is contained in some maximal
ideal m of A. If x ∈ m then xy ∈ m and this would imply that (1 − xy) + xy ∈ m, which is a
contradiction. Hence x ∈/ m, and x ∈/ R.
For the other inclusion we prove the contrapositive. Suppose that x ∈/ R. Then there is a
maximal ideal m such that x ∈/ m. It follows that m + (x) = A, which means there exists y ∈ A
such that 1 − xy ∈ m, so 1 − xy is not a unit. (cid:3)

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
label: The radical of an ideal. If a ⊆ A is an ideal, we define the radical of a to be
body:
r(a) := {x ∈ A : xn ∈ a for some n > 0}
If φ : A → A/a is the usual quotient map, then r(a) = φ−1 (cid:0) N (cid:1) , so r(a) is an ideal. Applying
A/a
Krull’s theorem to A/a we obtain the following corollary.
(cid:92)

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
body:
Let a ⊆ A be an ideal. Then r(a) = p.
p prime
a⊆p
Radicals enjoy a few properties that are easy to prove and are left for you to work out:
(1) r (r(a)) = r(a).
(2) r(ab) = r(a ∩ b) = r(a) ∩ r(b).
(3) r(a) = A ⇐⇒ a = A.
(4) r(a + b) = r (r(a) + r(b)).
(5) if p is prime then r(pn) = p.

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
label: The Chinese remainder theorem
body:
In elementary number theory you probably came across the Chinese remainder theorem. It says
that if m , . . . , m are pairwise relatively prime integers, and if a , . . . , a are any old integers, then
1 n 1 n
there exists an integer x satisfying the following simultaneous congruences:
x ≡ a mod m
1 1
.
.
.
x ≡ a mod m
n n
Put differently, given m , . . . , m pairwise relatively prime integers, the product map of quotient
1 n
maps
Z → Z/m Z × · · · × Z/m Z
1 n
x (cid:55)→ (x mod m , . . . , x mod m )
1 n
is surjective. This way of phrasing the theorem can be generalized to arbitrary commutative rings
with unit A. The theorem you know is the case A = Z. But we need a notion of “relatively prime”
first. Recall that two integers m and n are relatively prime if and only if there exist integers a and
b such that
am + bn = 1,
2
in other words, using ideals, if and only if (m) + (n) = (1). This is the notion that generalizes
nicely.

title: Lecture 3: 1. Nilradical and Jacobson radical (continued)
body:
Two ideals a and b of a ring A are relatively prime if a + b = (1).
It is easy to see that if a and b are any two ideals of a ring A then
(a + b)(a ∩ b) ⊆ ab
On the other hand, ab ⊆ a ∩ b and so
a ∩ b = ab provided a + b = (1).
So for relatively prime ideals we have a ∩ b = ab. More generally, we have the following Lemma.
(cid:81)

If a , . . . , a are pairwise coprime ideals of a ring A, then ∩a = a .
